note: EUR-Lex CELEX 32023R1542 resolves and is the correct official landing page for Regulation (EU) 2023/1542. The regulation is confirmed as chemistry-neutral and explicitly covers all battery chemistries including Na-ion. Carbon footprint declaration for industrial batteries >2 kWh mandatory from 18 February 2026 is confirmed by multiple authoritative sources (EUR-Lex summary, TUV Rheinland, digiprodpass.com). The delegated act on CFD calculation methodology: JRC Report JRC141282 (May 2025) was published as EU Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) guidance, confirming activity on this front, though the UNVERIFIED flag on the formal OJ delegated-act publication is appropriately retained. The row's gap assessment (no Chinese mandatory CFD equivalent) is accurate.

Same source URL as battreg-01 (CELEX 32023R1542) — resolves correctly. CE marking and conformity assessment obligations under 2023/1542 arts 10, 18-24 and the chemistry-neutral scope (art. 2) are confirmed. The row accurately flags that harmonised EN standards under the Battery Regulation for Na-ion are not yet in OJ. The dual-challenge description (CE requirement in force but harmonised standards not yet available for Na-ion) is accurate and well-caveated with UNVERIFIED flags. No errors found.

Source URL (CELEX 32025R1561) is confirmed: Regulation (EU) 2025/1561 exists (CELEX 32025R1561), published in OJ on 30 July 2025, amends Art 48(1) of 2023/1542, postponing the supply chain due diligence obligation from 18 August 2025 to 18 August 2027. The mandatory_status and summary accurately reflect this postponement. Labelling dates: basic separate-collection symbol from 18 August 2025, Part A physical label from 18 August 2026, QR code from 18 February 2027 — all confirmed. Battery Passport from 18 February 2027 confirmed. Note: the fragment text says 'basic labelling mandatory from 18 August 2025, extended labelling from 18 August 2026 or 18 months after the relevant implementing act, whichever is later' — this is a slight simplification but not an error; the 18 Aug 2025 date covers only the separate-collection symbol, while Part A labels are from 18 Aug 2026 or 18 months after implementing act — the fragment's phrasing is broadly correct.

Source URL (EUR-Lex CELEX 32014L0035 for LVD 2014/35/EU) resolves and is correct. IEC 62619:2022 confirmed to exist at IEC webstore (pub/64073, edition 2.0, May 2022, stability date 2026, TC21/SC21A). Title confirmed as 'Safety requirements for secondary lithium cells and batteries for use in industrial applications' — lithium-only in title and scope; Na-ion is NOT within explicit scope. The fragment correctly flags this with [UNVERIFIED] and recommends notified-body confirmation before using for Na-ion CE marking. URL FIX APPLIED: old URL (webstore.nsf/0/B8A5E2C3E9C8BF7DC12588D3004C6A7E) returned HTTP 403 and was a non-canonical hash-based URL; replaced with canonical IEC webstore URL https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/64073 in the fragment. Note: IEC 62984-4 is an emerging Na-ion-specific standard series from IEC TC21 but is not yet adopted as a harmonised EU standard; fragment does not mention it but this is not an error given the focus on EU compliance.

Source URL (EUR-Lex CELEX 32023R1542) resolves correctly. Standard EXISTS: IEC 62933-5-2 is confirmed at IEC webstore pub/68297. However, two issues: (1) EDITION ERROR — the fragment references IEC 62933-5-2:2020 (edition 1.0) but edition 2.0 was published December 2025 and supersedes the 2020 version. The fragment's title should reference :2025 edition. (2) URL FIX APPLIED: old URL (webstore.nsf/0/IEC62933-5-2) returned HTTP 403; replaced in fragment with canonical URL https://webstore.iec.ch/en/publication/68297 which confirmed as current edition 2.0 (2025). (3) The chemistry-agnostic claim is UNCERTAIN: ed. 2.0 scope lists Li-ion, lead-acid, nickel, flow, Li metal solid-state batteries plus an 'other technologies' clause — Na-ion is not explicitly named. The 2020 ed. 1.0 specifically listed 'high temperature sodium batteries' (i.e. NaS/ZEBRA, not room-temperature Na-ion). Room-temperature Na-ion may fall under 'other technologies' but this is not confirmed. The fragment's claim that the standard is 'more directly applicable to Na-ion than EN IEC 62619' is likely but remains unconfirmed pending explicit scope confirmation in ed. 2.0. The UNVERIFIED flags in the fragment appropriately cover this. standard_ok set false due to edition version mismatch (:2020 vs current :2025).

Source URL (unece.org/transport/dangerous-goods/un-model-regulations-rev-23) is the correct official UNECE landing page for the 23rd revised edition — confirmed by UNECE search results; the page returns HTTP 403 in automated fetch (likely geo/bot restriction) but is confirmed as the correct official URL. UN 3551 and UN 3552 are confirmed in ADR 2025 (Class 9, code M4, label 9A) with special provisions 188, 230, 310, 348, 376, 377, 400, 401, 636, 677 — confirmed by adr-tool.com (ADR official tool). UN Model Regs 23rd ed. (2023) origin confirmed by multiple sources. UN 38.3 requirement for Na-ion under these entries is confirmed. ADR 2025 effective date 1 January 2025 with transition period confirmed. IMDG Code Amendment 42-24 incorporating these entries confirmed. The fragment's description of the requirement is accurate.

Source URL same as transport-001 — confirmed correct official UNECE page. UN 3551 (Na-ion batteries with organic electrolyte, standalone) and UN 3552 (Na-ion batteries contained in or packed with equipment) confirmed in ADR 2025 as Class 9, code M4, no packing group. The fragment's UNVERIFIED flag on these numbers is now resolvable: multiple authoritative industry sources (ADR tool, Flexport regulatory blog, Boxlab Services, IATA guidance) consistently confirm UN 3551/3552 from UN Model Regs 23rd ed./ADR 2025. The 0V transport advantage claim: the fragment's chemistry rationale (Al vs Cu current collector enabling 0V stability in Na-ion) is technically accurate; the flag that this is not yet codified as a formal regulatory exemption in ADR/IMDG text is appropriate and unresolved — retain UNVERIFIED flag. The fragment's statement that the 23rd edition was published August 2023 is confirmed (UNECE: released 10 August 2023). Mandatory_status is correct.

Source URL (EUR-Lex CELEX 32023R1542) confirmed. LVD 2014/35/EU (CELEX 32014L0035) confirmed at EUR-Lex. EMC Directive 2014/30/EU referenced is confirmed as existing. Reg 2023/1542 CE marking requirement (Arts 17-20) confirmed as chemistry-neutral. The description of Module A (Decision 768/2008/EC) for conformity assessment is correct. EN IEC 62619:2022 listed as supporting standard — this is accurate (it is referenced for industrial battery CE marking under LVD) though Na-ion scope caveat appropriately mentioned. Mandatory_status correctly describes CE marking as mandatory before EU market entry. Gap analysis (CCC not equivalent to CE marking) is accurate. No errors found.

Source URL (EUR-Lex CELEX 32019R1020) confirmed as existing and resolving — Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 on market surveillance and compliance of products, including authorised representative obligations (Art. 4-5). Battery Regulation 2023/1542 Arts 56-61 on extended producer responsibility, registration and take-back confirmed. WEEE Directive 2012/19/EU Arts 16-17 on producer registration confirmed. The gap analysis (no Chinese domestic equivalent for EU authorised representative or EPR producer registration) is accurate. The UNVERIFIED flag on the exact definitional scope (industrial vs stationary battery under Art 3 of 2023/1542) is appropriate given pending implementing acts. Mandatory_status is correct.
